aliases: Broken Rice with Tofu and Mushroom

english: Broken Rice with Tofu and Mushroom

region_of_origin: Singapore (Vietnamese-origin dish adapted for Singapore market)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for vegetarian broken rice (com tam chay). FOODON_00002694 (rice dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (rice-based dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for vegetarian broken rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific dish. No suitable parent code identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of vegetarian broken rice, approximately 400 g, comprising broken rice, sauteed mushroom, tofu, vegetarian mix, one sunny-side-up egg, scallion oil, fried shallots, cucumber, pickled vegetables, and vegetarian dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: HPB Singapore Foodo (broken rice, egg, tofu, mushroom, vegetables) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (scallion oil, shallots, cucumber)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for vegetarian broken rice (com tam chay) found in HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values estimated by weighted component analysis: broken rice (~55% by weight), tofu and vegetarian mix (~15%), egg (~10%), mushroom and vegetables (~12%), scallion oil and fried shallots (~5%), sauce and pickle (~3%). Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on cooked white rice (GI ~64) adjusted downward for broken rice grain structure, tofu, egg, and vegetable components which lower the composite meal GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for vegetarian broken rice (com tam chay). Composite meal GI estimated at 50 using the University of Sydney GI Database reference for cooked white rice (GI 64, tested) as the primary carbohydrate source, adjusted downward by approximately 14 GI units to account for: (1) broken rice grain structure producing slower starch digestion than intact long-grain rice; (2) presence of tofu (high protein, low GI) contributing approximately 15% of plate weight; (3) egg protein further blunting glycemic response; (4) dietary fibre from mushroom, cucumber, and pickled vegetables. Composite meal GI estimation methodology follows FAO/WHO 1998 mixed-meal GI calculation guidance. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (50 × 86 g) / 100 = 43.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from dipping sauce and pickled vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce and limit pickled vegetable portions, as the dipping sauce and pickle components contribute approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g, equating to roughly 1240 mg sodium per standard 400 g plate, which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from broken rice with low composite GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: The composite GI of approximately 50 (low category) is favourable compared to plain white rice; however, the glycemic load per standard 400 g serving is approximately 43, which is high, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a reduced portion of rice (200–250 g plate) and prioritise the tofu, egg, mushroom, and vegetable components to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, plant-forward protein sources, and vegetable components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is relatively favourable for cardiovascular health due to its low saturated fat content (approximately 0.9 g per 100 g), plant-based protein from tofu, and the presence of mushroom and cucumber; however, the scallion oil and fried shallots contribute additional fat and calories, and sodium from the sauce should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Broken Rice (Cơm Tấm Chay) is a plant-forward adaptation of the classic Vietnamese broken rice dish (cơm tấm), served in Singapore at Vietnamese-style restaurants. The dish is built on a base of broken rice — the fragmented grains produced during milling that cook to a slightly softer, more porous texture than whole long-grain rice — which gives the dish its characteristic name and mouthfeel. The vegetarian version replaces the traditional grilled pork chop and pork skin with sautéed mushrooms, a vegetarian protein mix, and firm tofu, providing a satisfying umami depth without meat. A sunny-side-up egg is placed on top of the rice, adding richness and additional protein. The dish is finished with fragrant scallion oil drizzled over the rice, crispy fried shallots for texture and aroma, and served alongside fresh cucumber slices, pickled vegetables (daikon and carrot), and a homemade vegetarian dipping sauce (nước chấm chay) made without fish sauce. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a balanced macronutrient profile: carbohydrates from broken rice as the primary energy source, plant protein from tofu and vegetarian mix supplemented by egg protein, and moderate fat from scallion oil and egg yolk. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low category), making it more glycemically favourable than plain white rice dishes. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the dipping sauce and pickled vegetables, which is a clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians and is dairy-free, but is not vegan due to the egg. It is a culturally significant dish in Singapore's Vietnamese food scene, commonly found at Vietnamese-style eateries and hawker-adjacent restaurants.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for vegetarian broken rice (com tam chay) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any other primary approved source. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted component analysis using HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual ingredients. GI was estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database reference for cooked white rice, adjusted for the composite meal composition. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are approved and the estimation methodology is systematic and documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no exact dish-level entry was found in HPB Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for vegetarian broken rice (com tam chay). A direct measurement from a laboratory analysis of this specific dish would be required to upgrade confidence to high. 2. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in plant-based and minimally processed ingredients used; no industrial partially hydrogenated oils are present in this dish. 4. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g is based on a typical Vietnamese vegetarian dipping sauce formulation and pickled vegetable contribution; actual sodium will vary significantly by restaurant recipe. 5. The dish is served at Co Chung Restaurant, Lorong Telok, Singapore, as recorded in the field survey data. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component analysis and are not individually confirmed by a single primary source entry for this dish. 7. Price data from field survey data: SGD 20.58 (single restaurant, single price point). 8. Halal status is not confirmed; the restaurant is not listed as halal-certified in the source data.
